summary: DeVault introduces the Helios microkernel, a new operating system project written in Hare that draws heavily from seL4's capability-based design. The project targets x86_64 with riscv64 and aarch64 planned, and already has basic memory management, virtual address spaces, and simple userspace drivers working. The full vision extends beyond the kernel to a complete OS stack named after celestial bodies, from low-level services (Mercury) up to a GUI environment (Ares). DeVault frames the project as both genuinely fun and practically motivated, noting that even partial completion will yield useful tooling like a Hare debugger with DWARF support and a portable GUI toolkit.
key_points: Helios is a capability-based microkernel written entirely in Hare, inspired by seL4 but with the explicit goal of building a full userspace on top
The OS stack is organized as a solar system metaphor — Helios (kernel), Mercury (services), Venus (drivers), Gaia (programming environment), Ares (complete OS with GUI)
Real-world hardware usability is a core goal, with continuous testing on ThinkPads and plans for Intel graphics, audio, and Ethernet drivers
Even if the full stack never materializes, the project will produce useful byproducts: a Hare debugger with DWARF support, a GUI toolkit, and a standalone microkernel platform
money_quotes: I haven't had this much fun hacking in some time
This is a hugely ambitious project, or, in other words, my favorite kind of project.
Even if it's not ultimately useful, it will drive the development of a lot of useful stuff.
Helios and Mercury together have a reasonably small scope and makes for an interesting and useful platform in their own right, even if the rest of the stack never completely materializes.
If nothing else, it will probably be able to run DOOM fairly soon.
Why build a new operating system? Well, for a start, it's really fun.
key_insight: A capability-based microkernel written in Hare can serve as both a serious OS foundation and a driver for producing broadly useful systems tooling, even if the full OS vision is never fully realized.
